expectations.text: List endpoint is GET /api/tasks (plural, resource-oriented, no verbs)
expectations.evidence: Section '1. List tasks' shows '**`GET /api/tasks`**'

expectations.text: List response wraps rows in an envelope with `data` and a pagination sub-object (not a bare array, not a bespoke envelope like {tasks, total, limit, offset}, not a differently-named sub-object like pageInfo)
expectations.evidence: Sample response shows '"data": [...]' and '"pagination": { "nextCursor": ..., "hasMore": true }'; Paginated<T> type defined as { data: T[]; pagination: { nextCursor, hasMore } }

expectations.text: Pagination uses cursor-based pagination with default limit 20 and max limit 100 clamped server-side
expectations.evidence: Query parameter table: 'cursor' opaque string, 'limit ... Default 20, max 100. Server clamps; out-of-range rejected with InvalidRequestParam'

expectations.text: Search uses a single `q` query param
expectations.evidence: Query parameter table: '`q` | string | Free-text search across `title` and `description`'

expectations.text: Sort uses field:direction syntax on a single `sort` query param (e.g., `sort=priority:desc,createdAt:asc`) — not leading-minus syntax
expectations.evidence: '`sort` ... `field:direction` pairs, comma-separated. Allowed fields: `priority`, `createdAt`. Direction: `asc` | `desc`. ... Example: `sort=priority:desc,createdAt:asc`'

expectations.text: The 'complete' operation is expressed as a state transition via PATCH on the task resource (PATCH /api/tasks/:id with { status: 'COMPLETED' }) — NOT as a sub-resource action verb like POST /api/tasks/:id/complete
expectations.evidence: Section '4. Mark a task complete' shows '**`PATCH /api/tasks/:id`** ... To complete: PATCH /api/tasks/task_01HXY... { "status": "COMPLETED" }' and explicitly states 'There is no /complete sub-resource'

expectations.text: Side effects of completion (emails, audit rows) are described as service-layer responsibilities keyed to the state transition itself, not as a reason to invent a dedicated URL
expectations.evidence: Side-effect discipline section: 'Side effects ... attach to the actual transition, not to the URL' with code showing isCompletion check (old !== 'COMPLETED' && next === 'COMPLETED') gating sendCompletionEmail and audit.write

expectations.text: POST /api/tasks returns 201 Created with a Location header pointing at the new resource
expectations.evidence: Section '2. Create a task' Status codes: '`201 Created` — body is the new `Task`. Response includes `Location: /api/tasks/<id>` header.'

expectations.text: Wire format is camelCase for body/query fields (assigneeId, createdAt, etc.), not snake_case
expectations.evidence: Task interface: 'assigneeId: UserId | null; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; completedAt'; cross-cutting requirements: 'body fields and query params are camelCase'

expectations.text: TypeScript types separate input (CreateTaskInput) from output (Task) — output includes server-generated fields like id, createdAt
expectations.evidence: CreateTaskInput interface has only client-supplied fields (title, description?, priority?, assigneeId?, status?); Task interface separately defined with server-generated id, createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt, createdBy

expectations.text: Error envelope is FLAT at the top level — { code, message, details?, requestId } — NOT nested under an `error:` key
expectations.evidence: APIError interface: '{ code: string; message: string; details?: ...; requestId: string }' and explicit note 'There is no { error: { ... } } wrapper'

expectations.text: Error `code` values are domain-expressive (e.g., TaskNotFound, TaskAlreadyCompleted, InvalidRequestBody) and do NOT merely echo the HTTP status (not NOT_FOUND, not VALIDATION_ERROR, not CONFLICT as a generic code)
expectations.evidence: Error catalog table includes TaskNotFound, InvalidStatusTransition, TaskAlreadyCompleted, InvalidRequestBody, IdempotencyKeyReused, SessionExpired, InsufficientPermissions — explicitly states 'Codes are domain-expressive — none of them just echo the HTTP status word'

expectations.text: Catches snake_case on the wire (user_id, created_at, password_hash) and prescribes camelCase at the boundary
expectations.evidence: Finding 4: 'Wire format is snake_case (`user_id`, `created_at`, `password_hash`) — High'; correct alternative shows camelCase response with id, createdAt, updatedAt; mentions the case map at the route adapter

expectations.text: Catches verb in URL (/api/createUser) and proposes POST /api/users
expectations.evidence: Finding 2: '`POST /api/createUser` puts a verb in the URL'; correct alternative '**`POST /api/users`**'

expectations.text: Catches GET used for destructive action (/api/users/:id/delete) and proposes DELETE /api/users/:id
expectations.evidence: Finding 5: 'GET /api/users/:id/delete for a destructive action — Critical'; correct alternative 'DELETE /api/users/user_01HX...'

expectations.text: Catches the raw-array response from getUsers and proposes a paginated envelope
expectations.evidence: Finding 1: 'GET /api/users returns a raw array — High'; alternative shows '{ data, pagination: { nextCursor, hasMore } }'

expectations.text: Catches the bespoke listEvents envelope ({events,total,limit,offset}) and proposes the canonical shape
expectations.evidence: Finding 7: 'Bespoke list envelope `{ events, total, limit, offset }`'; alternative '{ data: [...], pagination: { nextCursor, hasMore } }' — same canonical envelope as users

expectations.text: Catches validation reporting one field at a time and prescribes returning all fields at once in details[]
expectations.evidence: Finding 8: 'Validation reports one field at a time'; alternative shows details: [{field, message}, {field, message}] array form

expectations.text: Catches POST returning 200 and prescribes 201 Created + Location header
expectations.evidence: Finding 3: 'POST returns 200 with no Location header — Medium'; alternative shows '201 Created' with 'Location: /api/users/user_01HX...'

expectations.text: Notes lack of X-Request-ID on responses (or absence of the correlating requestId in error bodies)
expectations.evidence: Finding 11: 'No X-Request-ID on responses; error bodies have no requestId'; prescribes 'X-Request-ID: req_01HX...' on every response and matching requestId in error body

expectations.text: The proposed error envelope is FLAT with domain-expressive codes — does not wrap contents in { error: {...} }, does not echo HTTP status as the code
expectations.evidence: Finding 9: 'Error envelope is wrapped under error: {...}'; alternative shows flat top-level '{ code, message, details?, requestId }'. Finding 10 prescribes domain-expressive codes (UserNotFound, EmailAlreadyTaken, InvalidStatusTransition) over NOT_FOUND/CONFLICT/VALIDATION_ERROR

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend introducing a `/v1/` URL prefix — follows the One-Version Rule's 'no default version segment'
expectations.evidence: Section 'What I am NOT recommending': 'No /v1/ URL prefix. Default to no version segment; reserve /v2/ for the rare case where you need to ship an incompatible change...'

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend returning `total` on an unbounded list (no COUNT(*) recommendation) — recognizes the skill's 'never return total on unbounded sets' rule
expectations.evidence: Finding 1: 'Do not return total. A COUNT(*) on a growing table is expensive...'; Finding 7 calls out '`total` on an unbounded set' as a problem; explicit non-recommendation 'No `total` field on the events list'

expectations.text: Flags the URL exposing DB implementation (user_table_v2 referenced / DB naming leaking)
expectations.evidence: Finding 6: 'URL exposes DB implementation (`user_table_v2`) — Medium'; explanation that `/api/user_table_v2/query` leaks an internal physical-storage detail; alternative names domain resources not storage tables

expectations.text: Identifies the missing Idempotency-Key header as the primary gap
expectations.evidence: Opening line: 'the draft is missing an Idempotency-Key header on a money-moving POST. Without it, any client retry... risks creating a duplicate charge. This is the single most important thing to add'

expectations.text: Describes retry semantics: same key + same payload returns the original response exactly
expectations.evidence: 'Same key arrives again with the same payload... This is a retry. **Replay the original response byte-for-byte** — same status, same body, same Location header. The server must not process the payment twice.'

expectations.text: Describes 409 CONFLICT when the same key is reused with a different payload
expectations.evidence: 'Same key arrives with a different payload... Reject with: HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict' with body code 'IdempotencyKeyReused'

expectations.text: Recommends persisting the idempotency key alongside the payload hash + response for a retention window (mentions 24h or longer for financial writes)
expectations.evidence: Persistence table includes idempotency_key, request_hash (SHA-256), response_status, response_body, response_headers, created_at; retention section: 'At least 24 hours, and significantly longer for payments... For financial writes I'd default to at least 7 days'

expectations.text: Does not mistakenly green-light the draft without flagging the idempotency gap
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'the draft is missing an Idempotency-Key header' and the entire response is structured around closing that gap; closing TL;DR table starts with the gap

expectations.text: Error envelope in the revised contract is flat (top-level { code, message, details?, requestId }) with domain-expressive codes like `IdempotencyKeyReused` — not wrapped in `error:`, not `CONFLICT` as the code
expectations.evidence: 409 example shows flat top-level keys: code: 'IdempotencyKeyReused', message, details, requestId — not wrapped in error:; catalog includes IdempotencyKeyRequired, IdempotencyKeyReused, IdempotencyRequestInFlight (all domain-expressive)

expectations.text: Does NOT add cursor/offset/page pagination (recognizes this as bounded-by-policy, not a growing list)
expectations.evidence: Section 'No pagination': 'This is bounded by policy at 5 — it is not a growing list. Cursor / offset / page parameters don't belong here.'

expectations.text: Returns the canonical `{ data: [...] }` envelope — NOT a bespoke envelope with a resource-named key like `{ notifications: [...] }`, and does not invent extra top-level fields like unreadCount or fetchedAt unless explicitly requested
expectations.evidence: Response shape is `{ "data": [...] }`; explicit notes 'No top-level resource name (no { "notifications": [...] })' and 'No invented sibling fields. No unreadCount, no fetchedAt, no lastSeenId'

expectations.text: URL contains no verbs (no /getNotifications, /fetchRecent, /latest)
expectations.evidence: URL: 'GET /api/notifications/recent. Plural noun, no verbs — getNotifications, fetchRecent, latest would all bake the action into the URL'

expectations.text: Explicitly notes the bound is server-enforced (client cannot request more than 5)
expectations.evidence: 'Do not let the client request fewer than 5, or — much more importantly — more than 5. The bound is server-enforced and fixed.' and 'the server returns at most 5, period'

expectations.text: Notes that broadening the bound later would be a breaking change (Hyrum's Law / contract discipline) — OR explicitly warns against exposing a `limit` query param
expectations.evidence: Both: 'No limit / count / take query parameter. Do not let the client request... more than 5'; AND 'Broadening the bound is a breaking change. Going from "always 5" to "configurable, default 20" is not additive'; AND Hyrum's Law explicitly invoked

expectations.text: List endpoint is GET /api/tasks (plural, resource-oriented, no verbs)
expectations.evidence: Section '1. List tasks' shows '**`GET /api/v1/tasks`**' — plural, no verbs (the `/v1/` prefix is not a verb)

expectations.text: List response wraps rows in an envelope with `data` and a pagination sub-object (not a bare array, not a bespoke envelope like {tasks, total, limit, offset}, not a differently-named sub-object like pageInfo)
expectations.evidence: ListTasksResponse uses bespoke shape '{ tasks: Task[]; total: number; page: number; pageSize: number }' — top-level resource-named key 'tasks' instead of 'data', no nested pagination sub-object, includes 'total'

expectations.text: Pagination uses cursor-based pagination with default limit 20 and max limit 100 clamped server-side
expectations.evidence: Uses page/pageSize (offset-based), default pageSize 25 (not 20). Max 100 mentioned but strategy is page-number not cursor

expectations.text: Search uses a single `q` query param
expectations.evidence: Query param table uses 'search' not 'q'

expectations.text: Sort uses field:direction syntax on a single `sort` query param (e.g., `sort=priority:desc,createdAt:asc`) — not leading-minus syntax
expectations.evidence: Sort example: 'e.g. `-priority,createdAt` (leading `-` = desc)' — uses leading-minus syntax, not field:direction

expectations.text: The 'complete' operation is expressed as a state transition via PATCH on the task resource (PATCH /api/tasks/:id with { status: 'COMPLETED' }) — NOT as a sub-resource action verb like POST /api/tasks/:id/complete
expectations.evidence: Section '4. Mark task complete' uses '**`POST /api/v1/tasks/:id/complete`**' — exactly the sub-resource action verb anti-pattern, with rationale 'a dedicated endpoint... separating it from a generic update keeps that side-effect contract explicit'

expectations.text: Side effects of completion (emails, audit rows) are described as service-layer responsibilities keyed to the state transition itself, not as a reason to invent a dedicated URL
expectations.evidence: Side effects are explicitly used as the *justification* for a dedicated URL: 'Why a dedicated endpoint? Completion is a *distinct domain operation* — it sends emails, writes audit rows...'

expectations.text: POST /api/tasks returns 201 Created with a Location header pointing at the new resource
expectations.evidence: Section '2. Create a task' Status codes: '200 OK — created' (no 201, no Location header)

expectations.text: Wire format is camelCase for body/query fields (assigneeId, createdAt, etc.), not snake_case
expectations.evidence: Conventions: 'snake_case-free (camelCase on the wire)'; Task interface uses createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt, createdById

expectations.text: TypeScript types separate input (CreateTaskInput) from output (Task) — output includes server-generated fields like id, createdAt
expectations.evidence: CreateTaskRequest contains only client-supplied fields (title, description?, priority?, assigneeId?, status?); Task type separately defined with id, createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt, createdById

expectations.text: Error envelope is FLAT at the top level — { code, message, details?, requestId } — NOT nested under an `error:` key
expectations.evidence: ApiErrorResponse: '{ error: { code: string; message: string; fields?: ... } }' — explicitly nested under `error:` key

expectations.text: Error `code` values are domain-expressive (e.g., TaskNotFound, TaskAlreadyCompleted, InvalidRequestBody) and do NOT merely echo the HTTP status (not NOT_FOUND, not VALIDATION_ERROR, not CONFLICT as a generic code)
expectations.evidence: Validation error example uses 'code: "VALIDATION_ERROR"'; status code section references '404 NOT_FOUND' and '409 CONFLICT' as the codes — all echo HTTP status

expectations.text: Catches snake_case on the wire (user_id, created_at, password_hash) and prescribes camelCase at the boundary
expectations.evidence: Section 3 mentions snake_case is unusual and shows camelCase example (userName, createdAt). Hedged ('If you have any reason to keep snake_case... be consistent') but the camelCase recommendation is present

expectations.text: Catches verb in URL (/api/createUser) and proposes POST /api/users
expectations.evidence: Section 2: 'verb in the URL... `createUser` is the HTTP method's job (POST)'; fix: 'POST /v1/users'

expectations.text: Catches GET used for destructive action (/api/users/:id/delete) and proposes DELETE /api/users/:id
expectations.evidence: Section 4: 'DELETE /v1/users/:id with 204 No Content on success'

expectations.text: Catches the raw-array response from getUsers and proposes a paginated envelope
expectations.evidence: Section 1: 'A bare array is hard to evolve... Wrap the response in an envelope and add pagination from day one' with example `{ users, page, pageSize, total }`

expectations.text: Catches the bespoke listEvents envelope ({events,total,limit,offset}) and proposes the canonical shape
expectations.evidence: Section 5: 'Bespoke per-endpoint envelopes are inconsistent... Pick a shape and use it everywhere' with example `{ data, pagination: { page, pageSize, total } }`

expectations.text: Catches validation reporting one field at a time and prescribes returning all fields at once in details[]
expectations.evidence: Section 6: 'Returning only the first validation error means the user has to round-trip... Return all validation failures in one response' with fields object example

expectations.text: Catches POST returning 200 and prescribes 201 Created + Location header
expectations.evidence: Section 3: 'conventional success status for a POST that creates a resource is 201 Created with a Location header'; fix shows '201 Created' with 'Location: /v1/users/abc123'

expectations.text: Notes lack of X-Request-ID on responses (or absence of the correlating requestId in error bodies)
expectations.evidence: No mention of X-Request-ID, request correlation, or requestId field anywhere in the review

expectations.text: The proposed error envelope is FLAT with domain-expressive codes — does not wrap contents in { error: {...} }, does not echo HTTP status as the code
expectations.evidence: Summary recommends '`{ error: { code, message, fields? } }`' — explicitly wrapped under `error:`. Section 6 example shows code 'VALIDATION_ERROR' which echoes the HTTP 422 status

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend introducing a `/v1/` URL prefix — follows the One-Version Rule's 'no default version segment'
expectations.evidence: Section 2: 'consider versioning the prefix (/v1/) so you have a clear migration path later'; final summary: 'Adopting /v1/ as a prefix from the start gives you room to evolve' — explicitly recommends /v1/

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend returning `total` on an unbounded list (no COUNT(*) recommendation) — recognizes the skill's 'never return total on unbounded sets' rule
expectations.evidence: Section 1 recommends `{ users, page, pageSize, total }`; Section 5 recommends `{ data, pagination: { page, pageSize, total } }` — both include `total`

expectations.text: Flags the URL exposing DB implementation (user_table_v2 referenced / DB naming leaking)
expectations.evidence: No mention of user_table_v2 or any URL exposing DB schema/implementation

expectations.text: Identifies the missing Idempotency-Key header as the primary gap
expectations.evidence: The review covers auth, validation, error responses, PCI, logging, rate limiting, async webhooks, and status codes — but never mentions Idempotency-Key, idempotency, or duplicate-charge protection on retries

expectations.text: Describes retry semantics: same key + same payload returns the original response exactly
expectations.evidence: No discussion of retry semantics or idempotency key handling

expectations.text: Describes 409 CONFLICT when the same key is reused with a different payload
expectations.evidence: Status codes mention 422, 402, 429 but no 409 in the context of idempotency-key reuse

expectations.text: Recommends persisting the idempotency key alongside the payload hash + response for a retention window (mentions 24h or longer for financial writes)
expectations.evidence: No mention of persisting idempotency keys, payload hashes, or retention windows

expectations.text: Does not mistakenly green-light the draft without flagging the idempotency gap
expectations.evidence: Summary closes with 'Beyond that the shape looks fine' — effectively green-lighting the contract while missing the central money-moving gap

expectations.text: Error envelope in the revised contract is flat (top-level { code, message, details?, requestId }) with domain-expressive codes like `IdempotencyKeyReused` — not wrapped in `error:`, not `CONFLICT` as the code
expectations.evidence: Section 3 example wraps payload in '{ error: { code, message } }'; no IdempotencyKeyReused code present (the topic is absent entirely)

expectations.text: Does NOT add cursor/offset/page pagination (recognizes this as bounded-by-policy, not a growing list)
expectations.evidence: No cursor, offset, or page parameters in the design; opening line 'Since the requirement is fixed at 5 and there's no pagination, you can keep this very simple'

expectations.text: Returns the canonical `{ data: [...] }` envelope — NOT a bespoke envelope with a resource-named key like `{ notifications: [...] }`, and does not invent extra top-level fields like unreadCount or fetchedAt unless explicitly requested
expectations.evidence: Response shape is bespoke: '{ notifications: [...], unreadCount: 3, fetchedAt: "..." }' — uses resource-named key 'notifications' and invents both unreadCount AND fetchedAt extras (the exact two fields the assertion warns against)

expectations.text: URL contains no verbs (no /getNotifications, /fetchRecent, /latest)
expectations.evidence: Recommends 'GET /api/notifications/recent' — no verbs

expectations.text: Explicitly notes the bound is server-enforced (client cannot request more than 5)
expectations.evidence: Closing line: 'Keep the array length capped at 5 server-side regardless of any client query params' — explicitly server-enforced

expectations.text: Notes that broadening the bound later would be a breaking change (Hyrum's Law / contract discipline) — OR explicitly warns against exposing a `limit` query param
expectations.evidence: No mention that broadening would be a breaking change. Mentions 'or alternatively GET /api/notifications?limit=5' as an option (the opposite of warning against a limit param)
